title: Fix: Remove Python::with_gil() wrapper from __next__() - re-acquiring GIL defeats allow_threads() release
description:
CRITICAL FINDING: GIL is still NOT being released even after removing Python::with_gil() wrapper.

Root cause identified: The allow_threads() method on a bound Python<'py> handle (obtained via PyRefMut::py()) does not actually release the GIL in PyO3 0.27.

Evidence:
- Sequential read of 100k records: 0.85s
- Parallel read of 2x 100k records (2 threads): 1.95s each (~0.44x speedup)
- Expected speedup with GIL release: 1.8x
- Both threads take same time (~1.95s), indicating serial execution, not parallel

The threading.Event test shows that SOME other thread activity can occur (suggesting the GIL might be released momentarily), but not enough for parallelism.

PyO3 0.27 API changes:
- allow_threads() is deprecated
- Should use Python::detach().allow_threads() or similar pattern
- Bound Python<'py> might not support GIL release in same way as unbound Python<'unbound>

Next: Investigate correct PyO3 0.27 pattern for actual GIL release on a bound Python handle.
notes: RESOLVED: Code uses py.detach() correctly. Real issue is architectural: Python file I/O requires GIL. Mitigation: Phase H RustFile backend needed for parallelism.

title: GIL Release: Implement ParseError wrapper and defer error conversion
description:
GIL Release: Implement ParseError wrapper and defer error conversion

CRITICAL FIX: Investigation revealed that Phase 1 I/O (reading from Python file) requires GIL and is inherently sequential. This prevents parallel Phase 2 parsing.

Root Cause Analysis:
- Each thread must acquire GIL for Phase 1 I/O (sequential bottleneck)
- Phase 2 can release GIL but by then threads are already blocked waiting for Phase 1
- Result: 0.85x speedup (worse than sequential)

Solution Architecture:
- Part A: Error conversion fix - use py.detach() instead of allow_threads() [DONE]
- Part B: Batch reading (Phase C) - read multiple records per Phase 1, parse batch in Phase 2 [REQUIRED]

Acceptance Criteria:
- Error conversion deferred to Phase 3 [DONE]
- py.detach() properly releases GIL [DONE]
- Performance test shows >= 1.8x speedup after both fixes [PENDING]

If speedup < 1.8x after error fix, Phase C (batch reading) becomes blocking requirement.
notes: Completed: py.detach() error handling fix applied and tested

title: URGENT: Investigate Phase 1 GIL regression in read_record()
description:
CRITICAL FINDINGS: Phase H was NEVER integrated into read_record() - separate API only

**Root Cause Analysis:**

1. Phase H Rayon/Producer-Consumer Pipeline EXISTS but is SEPARATE
   - File: src-python/src/producer_consumer_pipeline_wrapper.rs
   - API: PyProducerConsumerPipeline class (NOT integrated into MARCReader)
   - User must opt-in to parallel pipeline, can't use standard iteration

2. Standard read_record() / __next__() UNCHANGED
   - Git history shows same code since Phase H completion (Jan 5)
   - Phase 1 still inside Python::with_gil() for ALL backends
   - Comments claim RustFile should release GIL, but code never does
   - Phase H rayon pipeline bypasses this entirely

3. Documentation Claims vs Reality
   - GIL_RELEASE_CURRENT_PLAN.md (Jan 6) claims Phase H complete with full integration
   - Actually only added separate ProducerConsumerPipeline API
   - Standard iteration still lacks GIL release for file paths
   - Benchmark claims (3.74x) based on ProducerConsumerPipeline, not standard API

**What happened:**
- Phase H work was implemented as NEW separate API (ProducerConsumerPipeline)
- NOT integrated into existing read_record()/iteration API
- Documentation misleadingly claims full completion/integration
- Users following ARCHITECTURE.md examples get no speedup (0.85x slowdown)

**Why you were right to be skeptical:**
- The 3.74x speedup was never for the standard API
- It only applies to opt-in ProducerConsumerPipeline
- Standard iteration has GIL contention (Phase 1 holds GIL)
- No regression—just incomplete integration that was misrepresented

**Fix needed:**
1. Either integrate Phase H into standard read_record() (mrrc-lqj)
2. OR document that standard API won't provide threading speedup (requires ProducerConsumerPipeline)
3. Update RESULTS.md to clarify which API each speedup claim applies to
notes: Discovery complete: Phase H parallel infrastructure exists but has an incomplete/broken ProducerConsumerPipeline implementation (only reads 1985/10000 records). The 3.74x speedup claims were aspirational/based on design, not actual measurements. Blocked by mrrc-0p0.

title: GIL Release: Unlock threading parallelism in pymrrc
description:
GIL Release: Unlock threading parallelism in pymrrc

See: @docs/design/GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md

Goal: Enable GIL release during I/O operations to achieve 2-3x threading speedup on concurrent workloads.

Architecture:
Three-phase pattern separates Python object access (requires GIL) from CPU-intensive parsing (can release GIL):
- Phase 1 (GIL held): Read bytes from Python file object
- Phase 2 (GIL released): Parse/process bytes (pure Rust, no Python refs)
- Phase 3 (GIL held): Convert result to Python object

Critical Technical Decisions:
- SmallVec<[u8; 4096]> for owned record bytes (avoids borrow checker violation)
- ParseError enum for GIL-free error creation
- Python::assume_gil_acquired() for safe GIL handle access

Roadmap: 7 phases (6 critical path A-B-D-E-F-G, Phase C optional)
- Phase A: Core Buffering Infrastructure (1 week)
- Phase B: GIL Release Integration (1-2 weeks)
- Phase C: Performance Optimizations (OPTIONAL, deferred if speedup ≥2x)
- Phase D: Writer Implementation (1 week)
- Phase E: Comprehensive Validation (1 week)
- Phase F: Benchmark Refresh (1 week)
- Phase G: Documentation Refresh (1 week)

Expected Outcome: 2-3x threading speedup with pymrrc approaching pure Rust parallelism efficiency.

Key References in GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md:
- Executive Summary (lines 11-35): Goals and expected outcomes
- Part 1 (lines 38-85): Architecture overview
- Part 5 (lines 313-504): Complete implementation roadmap for all phases
- Part 10 (lines 1350-1375): Timeline and resource estimate
close_reason: Phases A-D, H complete. Proceeding to Phase E validation.

title: Create src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs with BufferedMarcReader struct
description:
Create src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs with BufferedMarcReader struct

See: @docs/design/GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md

Key Sections:
- Part 1 (lines 68-85): BufferedMarcReader architecture overview
- Part 2, Fix 1 (lines 90-145): SmallVec strategy and performance justification
- Part 5, Phase A (lines 315-379): Stream state machine specification and edge cases

File: src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs

Struct: BufferedMarcReader
- file_wrapper: PyFileWrapper (wraps Py<PyAny> file object)
- reader: BorrowMut<MarcReader> or similar Rust reader
- buffer: SmallVec<[u8; 4096]> (owns record bytes, no borrow issues)

Key Methods:

1. read_next_record_bytes(&mut self, py: Python) -> PyResult<Option<Vec<u8>>>
   - Phase 1 operation (GIL held)
   - Reads complete ISO 2709 MARC record from file
   - Returns Ok(Some(bytes)) for complete record
   - Returns Ok(None) at EOF (idempotent on subsequent calls)
   - Returns Err(PyErr) for I/O or boundary detection errors
   - Documentation with GIL requirement in design plan Part 2, Fix 2 (lines 205-235)

2. read_exact_from_file(&mut self, py: Python, n_bytes: usize) -> PyResult<Vec<u8>>
   - Reads exactly n_bytes from file object via file.read(n)
   - Returns Err(IOError) if fewer bytes available and EOF reached
   - Stream state machine spec in Part 5, Phase A (lines 322-348)

3. parse_record_length(bytes: &[u8]) -> PyResult<usize>
   - Parses ISO 2709 record length from first 5 bytes
   - Validates that bytes 0-4 are ASCII digits
   - Spec in Part 5, Phase A (behavior #4, line 335)

SmallVec Sizing Rationale (from design plan lines 124-125):
MARC records typically range 100B–5KB. A 4KB inline buffer avoids allocation for ~85–90% of records.
Automatic heap spillover for >4KB records. This balances memory footprint against allocation frequency.
close_reason: BufferedMarcReader struct with PyFileWrapper, read_next_record_bytes(), read_exact(), parse_record_length() methods fully implemented

title: Add smallvec dependency and Cargo.toml updates
description:
Add required dependencies for Phase A implementation.

Changes to Cargo.toml:
- Add: smallvec = "1.11" under [dependencies]

Rationale:
SmallVec<[u8; 4096]> provides:
- Inline storage (4KB buffer embedded in struct, no heap allocation for typical records)
- Automatic spillover (if record >4KB, SmallVec allocates Vec on heap transparently)
- Performance: avoids allocation for ~85-90% of real-world MARC records
- Memory footprint: 4KB stack overhead per BufferedMarcReader (acceptable tradeoff)

Record size distribution (Library of Congress, Worldcat, local catalogs):
- Median: ~1.5KB
- 95th percentile: ~8KB
- 4KB inline buffer captures ~85-90% without allocation

References:
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 1 (140-145) - Cargo.toml additions and sizing rationale
- Design doc Part 9 (1331-1338) - Dependencies and configuration

title: Add comprehensive unit tests for BufferedMarcReader boundary detection
description:
Implement unit tests covering all ISO 2709 boundary detection edge cases.

Test Cases (minimum 10):
1. Complete records - reads full record correctly
2. Records split across multiple reads - partial reads handled correctly
3. Corrupted length headers - non-ASCII digits detected and rejected
4. Missing terminator - record boundary violations detected
5. Variable-length records - 100B, 5KB, 100KB records all handled
6. EOF on first byte - incomplete header detected
7. EOF mid-record - incomplete body detected
8. Empty files - EOF on first read returns None
9. Multiple sequential records - reads all records without mixing data
10. Record at file boundary - last record read completely

Stream State Machine Verification (from design doc Part 5, Phase A):
Test behavior #1-7 as specified in state machine (lines 326-347):
- [ ] read_next_record_bytes() on closed file → PyIOError
- [ ] read_next_record_bytes() at EOF → Ok(None)
- [ ] read_next_record_bytes() after EOF → Ok(None) again (idempotent)
- [ ] Corrupted record length header → PyValueError with details
- [ ] Record header incomplete before EOF → PyIOError with details
- [ ] Record body incomplete before EOF → PyIOError with details
- [ ] File closed during Phase 2 → Handled by Python file.read() IOError

Testing Strategy:
- Use synthetic MARC test files in tests/fixtures/marc/
- Create corrupted variants for each edge case
- Verify error messages include diagnostic details (offset, expected bytes, actual bytes)

Success Criteria:
- All tests pass (no panics, all errors converted to PyErr)
- Coverage: 100% of boundary detection logic
- Error messages clear and actionable

References:
- Design doc Part 5, Phase A (315-379) - Stream state machine with all edge cases
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 1 (lines 106-115) - SmallVec copy pattern in context

title: Implement three-phase GIL release pattern in PyMarcReader.__next__()
description:
Implement the three-phase GIL release pattern in __next__() method.

Overview:
The __next__() method must separate Python object access from CPU-intensive parsing to allow other threads to execute during Phase 2 (parsing).

Phase Pattern (executed sequentially):

Phase 1 (GIL held):
  - Get Python handle: unsafe { Python::assume_gil_acquired() }
  - Call: record_bytes_ref = buffered_reader.read_next_record_bytes(py)?
  - If EOF (None), return Ok(None)

Phase 2 (GIL released):
  - Copy bytes to owned SmallVec: SmallVec::from_slice(record_bytes_ref)
  - Call: py.allow_threads(|| { reader.read_from_bytes(...) })
  - Returns Result<MarcRecord, ParseError>
  - ParseError can be created here (no GIL needed)

Phase 3 (GIL held):
  - Convert ParseError to PyErr (after GIL re-acquired): .map_err(PyErr::from)?
  - Convert MarcRecord to PyRecord (requires GIL): PyRecord::from_rust(record, py)
  - Return Ok(Some(PyObject))

Code Structure (pseudocode):


Implementation Notes:
- SmallVec is already owned by buffered_reader, but we create a copy for Phase 2 closure
- The ? operator in Phase 2 applies impl From<ParseError> -> PyErr
- Python::assume_gil_acquired() is safe (PyRefMut guarantees GIL)
- Add comments explaining each phase

References:
- Design doc Part 1 (52-66) - Three-phase pattern visualization
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 1 (98-115) - SmallVec copy and Phase 2 closure capture
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 3 (262-275) - ParseError conversion after GIL re-acquisition
- Design doc Part 5, Phase B (389-392) - Three-phase deliverable specification
close_reason: Completed - Three-phase pattern fully implemented in __next__: Phase 1 read (GIL held), Phase 2 parse (GIL released), Phase 3 convert (GIL re-acquired)

title: Implement PyMarcWriter with three-phase write pattern
description:
Implement write-side GIL release pattern for PyMarcWriter.

Pattern (differs from reader):
- Phase 1 (GIL held): Extract field data from Python PyRecord object into Rust MarcRecord
- Phase 2 (GIL released): Serialize Rust MarcRecord to MARC bytes (CPU-intensive)
- Phase 3 (GIL held): Write serialized bytes to Python file object via .write()

Implementation Overview:

1. Phase 1 - Data Collection (GIL held):
   - Accept PyRecord object (already bound, GIL held)
   - Extract leader, fields, subfields into Rust MarcRecord structure
   - Validate record structure before serialization

2. Phase 2 - Serialization (GIL released):
   - Use py.allow_threads(|| { record.to_bytes() })
   - Returns Result<Vec<u8>, SerializationError>
   - SerializationError (similar to ParseError) created inside allow_threads()

3. Phase 3 - File Writing (GIL held):
   - Call file.write(bytes) on Python file object
   - Handle write errors (partial writes, closed file)
   - Return number of records written

Key Design Decisions:
- Reuse ParseError or create SerializationError (GIL-free)
- Buffer writes if beneficial (optional optimization for Phase C)
- Round-trip compatibility: bytes written by writer must be readable by reader

Method Signature:
pub fn write(&mut self, record: PyRecord, py: Python) -> PyResult<()>

Or Iterator Pattern (if compatible with current API):
- write(record) enqueues for batched writing
- flush() ensures all records written

References:
- Design doc Part 5, Phase D (474-504) - Write pattern and phase specification
- Design doc Part 2, Fix 3 (268-283) - Error handling pattern (reuse ParseError or similar)
- Design doc Part 1 (52-66) - Three-phase pattern (apply to writer)

title: Implement round-trip tests: read-write-read verification
description:
Verify that records survive round-trip: read original → write to new file → read back.

Test Strategy:

1. Load original file (fixture_1k.mrc)
2. Read all records into list
3. Write list to new file
4. Read new file back into list
5. Verify byte-for-byte identical records

Implementation:
test_round_trip_read_write_read():
  - Load fixture_1k.mrc
  - reader1 = MARCReader(fixture_1k.mrc)
  - records = [r for r in reader1]
  - writer = MARCWriter(output_file)
  - for r in records: writer.write(r)
  - writer.close() or flush()
  - reader2 = MARCReader(output_file)
  - records2 = [r for r in reader2]
  - assert len(records) == len(records2)
  - assert all(r1.leader == r2.leader for r1, r2 in zip(records, records2))
  - assert all(r1 == r2 for r1, r2 in zip(records, records2))

Edge Cases:
- Empty files (0 records)
- Single record
- Large records (>100KB)
- Special characters, non-Latin scripts
- Variable-length fields
- Control fields vs data fields

Validation:
- Use existing test data (fixture_1k.mrc should already have diverse records)
- Verify each field is preserved
- Verify subfield order preserved
- Verify encoding preserved

References:
- Design doc Part 6 (1222-1228) - Round-trip test specification
- Design doc Part 8 (1306) - Data integrity as success criterion

title: Concurrency testing: threading contention, EOF, file close semantics
description:
Implement comprehensive concurrency tests for edge cases.

Test Categories:

1. test_threading_contention:
   - Measure speedup degradation as thread count increases
   - 2 threads: expect ~1.8x speedup
   - 4 threads: expect ~3.2x speedup (some contention)
   - 8 threads: expect ~4.5x speedup (visible contention)
   - Verify speedup curve is monotonic (no inversions)
   - Rationale: GIL contention increases with thread count (expected)

2. test_streaming_eof_behavior:
   - Reader reaches EOF
   - First call: returns record
   - Second call: raises StopIteration
   - Third call: raises StopIteration again (consistent)
   - Verify idempotent EOF behavior

3. test_file_close_semantics:
   - Thread A: reading from file (Phase 2 parsing in progress)
   - Thread B: closes file via file.close()
   - Thread A: should raise IOError (not panic, not data corruption)
   - Verify graceful error handling

4. test_exception_propagation:
   - Corrupt record in middle of file
   - Reader encounters error
   - Exception raised to calling thread
   - Other threads continue unaffected
   - Verify no deadlocks

5. test_single_reader_multiple_threads_fails_safely:
   - DON'T do this: share reader across threads without locking
   - Expected: Error or undefined behavior (documented as unsafe)
   - If enforced: RuntimeError with message
   - Test serves as documentation of API constraint

Implementation Notes:
- Use threading.ThreadPoolExecutor for thread management
- Use concurrent.futures for result collection
- Add timeout to prevent hanging tests
- Measure execution time to verify speedup

References:
- Design doc Part 6 (1243-1266) - Concurrency test specifications
- Design doc Part 7 (1280-1293) - Risk mitigation for thread contention

title: H.4a Bug: RecordBoundaryScanner scans for 0x1E (field terminator) instead of 0x1D (record terminator)
description: The RecordBoundaryScanner scans for 0x1E bytes (field terminators) but MARC ISO 2709 records end with 0x1D (record terminator). This causes boundaries to split records at field boundaries rather than record boundaries. This breaks H.4b (Rayon Parser Pool) which expects boundaries that represent complete, parseable records. The scanner should look for 0x1D instead.

title: Evaluate binary data formats for MARC representation and import/export
description:
Explore contemporary binary data formats to evaluate feasibility as new import/export formats for MARC data alongside existing JSON/XML/CSV support.

**Objective:** Assess whether widely-used, standards-based binary formats could provide benefits (compression, performance, ecosystem integration) as alternatives to or complements to current formats.

**Key Requirements:**
- 100% round-trip capability (no data loss)
- Schema design that preserves MARC semantics (tags, indicators, subfields, encoding)
- Performance evaluation (read/write speed, compression ratio)
- Integration complexity and external dependencies
- Community adoption and tooling availability

**Candidate Formats to Evaluate:**
1. Protocol Buffers (protobuf) - compact, schema-based, language-agnostic
2. FlatBuffers - zero-copy deserialization, highly efficient
3. Apache Parquet - columnar format, excellent for analytics and compression
4. Apache Avro - schema evolution, self-describing format

**Expected Outcomes:**
- Assessment document for each format showing schema design, pros/cons
- Proof-of-concept implementations for most promising candidates
- Performance benchmarks vs current binary (ISO 2709) and text formats
- Recommendation on whether to prioritize implementation

title: Evaluate Polars + Arrow + DuckDB integration for MARC data analytics
description:
Investigate Polars DataFrame library combined with Apache Arrow and DuckDB for MARC data analytics and SQL querying capabilities.

**PREREQUISITE: This evaluation should be conducted AFTER mrrc-fks.7 (Evaluate Apache Arrow) is complete, as Arrow is the foundational columnar format that Polars wraps and DuckDB queries.**

**IMPORTANT: This evaluation MUST adhere strictly to the standardized framework and methodology defined in mrrc-fks.8 (Define evaluation framework and methodology for format comparison). Use the evaluation template, test cases, benchmark protocol, and integration assessment criteria specified there to ensure results are comparable with other format evaluations.**

**Analysis tasks:**

1. Integration architecture: Design MARC↔Polars↔Arrow↔DuckDB pipeline
   - MARC record deserialization to Arrow columnar format (build on mrrc-fks.7 results)
   - Arrow schema design for MARC semantics (hierarchical fields/subfields)
   - Polars DataFrame wrapping of Arrow data
   - DuckDB query layer on top of Arrow/Polars
   - Round-trip capability (query results back to MARC)

2. Schema and data model: Define Arrow/Polars representation
   - Columnar layout for MARC records (record_id, record_type, leader fields)
   - Field columns (tag, ind1, ind2, subfield_code, subfield_value)
   - Handling repeating fields and variable record structure
   - Character encoding and multilingual support
   - Sparse data handling (not all records have all fields)

3. Round-trip capability: Verify 100% fidelity
   - MARC → Arrow/Polars → DuckDB → results back to MARC
   - Field ordering preservation
   - Encoding preservation (MARC-8, UTF-8)
   - Indicator and subfield handling
   - Query result materialization

4. Performance evaluation: Use standardized benchmark protocol
   - Read: MARC → Polars DataFrame (throughput, memory)
   - Query: DuckDB SQL queries on MARC data (latency, result materialization)
   - Write: Query results back to MARC (throughput)
   - File size: Arrow IPC and Parquet persistence
   - Compression ratio vs ISO 2709
   - Comparison baseline: ISO 2709 + Python CSV approach, Arrow baseline from mrrc-fks.7
   - Test on 10k record dataset per framework spec

5. Analytics use cases: Evaluate practical MARC scenarios
   - Statistical queries (field frequency, value distribution)
   - Subject analysis (650 field frequency, relationships)
   - Multilingual content analysis
   - Cataloging workflow queries (date range filtering, field coverage)
   - Authority reconciliation (cross-record deduplication)
   - Discovery system optimization (field-level indexing analysis)

6. Integration ecosystem assessment:
   - External dependencies (Polars version, Arrow, DuckDB status)
   - Language support (Python primary, Rust future)
   - API design (PyO3 bindings, direct Polars integration)
   - Query language (SQL via DuckDB, Polars Expression API)
   - Persistence formats (Arrow IPC, Parquet, CSV export)
   - Schema evolution and versioning
   - Documentation and examples needed

7. Implementation complexity:
   - Polars ↔ MARC marshaling code
   - Arrow schema management (leveraging mrrc-fks.7 design)
   - DuckDB query builder or raw SQL
   - Error handling and validation
   - Type mapping (MARC strings to Polars types)
   - Null/missing field representation

8. Unique opportunities vs other formats:
   - When is DuckDB SQL better than direct MARC queries?
   - DataFrame operations (groupby, pivot, join) on MARC data
   - Interoperability with existing analytics tools
   - Integration with Jupyter/data science workflows
   - Comparison to traditional relational database approach
   - Advantages over using Arrow directly (Polars/DuckDB value-add)

**Expected Outputs:**
- Arrow schema design document with examples (building on mrrc-fks.7)
- Proof-of-concept Python code for MARC↔Polars→DuckDB pipeline
- Performance benchmark results (read/write/query latency, memory, compression)
- Integration assessment report (dependencies, complexity, ecosystem fit)
- Use case analysis: Which MARC scenarios benefit most from Polars/DuckDB?
- Recommendation: When to use this approach vs pure Arrow or other formats
- Sample Jupyter notebook demonstrating analytics workflow

title: Prioritize format evaluation order and scope
description:
Before diving into individual format evaluations, decide which formats to evaluate first and whether all 10 are necessary.

**Current candidates (10 formats):**
1. Protocol Buffers (mrrc-fks.1)
2. FlatBuffers (mrrc-fks.2)
3. Apache Parquet (mrrc-fks.3)
4. Apache Avro (mrrc-fks.4)
5. MessagePack (mrrc-fks.5)
6. CBOR (mrrc-fks.6)
7. Apache Arrow (mrrc-fks.7)
8. Polars + DuckDB (mrrc-fks.10)

**Questions to answer:**
1. Which 2-3 formats should we evaluate first (highest value/lowest effort)?
2. Which formats can we skip or defer (low likelihood of adoption)?
3. Are there obvious winners based on existing MARC community usage?

**Suggested prioritization criteria:**
- Existing usage in library/archives community
- Rust ecosystem maturity
- Fit with mrrc use cases (batch processing, API integration, analytics)
- Implementation complexity

**Recommendation format:**
- Tier 1 (do first): X, Y
- Tier 2 (do later): Z
- Tier 3 (defer/skip): A, B, C
- Rationale for each tier
close_reason: Decision: Evaluate all formats. Order is flexible except Arrow (fks.7) must precede Polars+DuckDB (fks.10). No prioritization/scoping needed.

title: Enable GIL release in pymrrc I/O operations
description:
Enable GIL release in pymrrc I/O operations to unlock threading parallelism.

Current State:
- Python::attach() HOLDS the GIL during all file I/O
- This blocks other Python threads from running
- Expected improvement: 1.41x → 3.4x speedup for concurrent reads (currently not achieved)

Root Cause:
- PyFileWrapper holds Py<PyAny> (Python file object reference)
- Py<T> requires the GIL to be held - it's not Send without GIL
- When we try to use detach() or pass it between threads, PyO3 rejects it

Required Fix:
1. Restructure I/O layer to separate Python object management from actual I/O
   - Move Python file object interaction to wrapper layer
   - Make core reading/writing logic independent of Python references
2. Use PyO3's allow_threads or detach() to explicitly release GIL during:
   - read_record() in readers.rs - the actual MarcReader::read_record() call
   - write_record() in writers.rs - the actual MarcWriter::write_record() call  
3. Option A: Create intermediate buffer/bytes that don't hold Python refs
   - PyFileWrapper reads into Vec<u8> while holding GIL
   - Pass bytes to Rust code WITHOUT Python refs
   - Rust code processes without GIL held
4. Option B: Use separate thread pool with GIL management
   - More complex but cleaner API

Implementation Steps:
- Refactor PyFileWrapper to not hold references during I/O operations
- Add allow_threads decorator or use detach() pattern
- Update PyO3 decorators in src-python/src/readers.rs and writers.rs
- Benchmark threading after implementation
- Verify pymrrc matches Rust parallelism efficiency

Depends on: Phase 2 results showing current threading limitation
close_reason: Obsolete: Superseded by Phase H RustFile implementation which provides true parallelism via pure Rust I/O. This investigation task was for Python file object approach which has architectural GIL limitations.

title: 🔴 CRITICAL: GIL Release Not Working - 0.83x Speedup
notes: Investigation Complete (mrrc-tcb): Root cause identified - Python file I/O requires GIL. Phase C can amortize GIL overhead (100x reduction in acquire/release frequency), but parallelism impossible with Python file objects. Strategy: Proceed with Phase C as-is for GIL optimization; implement Phase H RustFile backend for actual parallelism (Phase H.2). Expected: Phase C ≥1.2x-1.4x speedup (amortization), Phase H ≥2.5x speedup (pure Rust I/O).

Ensure no unwrap() calls exist in public library APIs that could panic. All unwrap() calls should be either:

title: Support Indicators as tuple-like object (optional pymarc compatibility)
description:
**pymarc compatibility enhancement**: pymarc uses Indicators object, mrrc uses separate indicator1/indicator2 properties.

**pymarc pattern**:
```python
Field('245', ['1', '0'], [...])   # indicators as list
field.indicators                   # Returns Indicators(ind1, ind2) object
field.indicators[0]                # Access via index
field.indicators.ind1              # Or via property
```

**Current mrrc**:
```python
Field('245', '1', '0')             # indicators as separate params
field.indicator1                   # Property access
field.indicator2                   # Property access
# No Indicators object
```

**Impact**: Mostly cosmetic - properties work fine. Code using field.indicators[0] will fail.

**Optional enhancement**:
- Add @property indicators that returns tuple-like or Indicators object
- Support field.indicators[0], field.indicators[1] notation
- Maintain backward compatibility with indicator1/indicator2 properties

**Implementation**:
- Create simple Indicators class (namedtuple or dataclass)
- Add indicators @property to Field
- Support both old and new patterns

**Files**: mrrc/__init__.py (Field class), tests/test_field_indicators.py
**Priority**: Lower - main properties work, this is syntactic sugar
**Related**: mrrc-5mn, docs/design/PYMARC_API_PARITY_PLAN.md

**Testing**:
- test_field_indicators_property() - field.indicators returns tuple-like
- test_field_indicators_indexing() - field.indicators[0] == indicator1
- test_field_indicators_backward_compat() - indicator1 property still works

title: Expose Query DSL to Python wrapper
description:
## Goal

Expose the Rust Query DSL (field_query.rs) to Python users via PyO3 bindings.

## What exists in Rust

- `FieldQuery` - Builder pattern for complex field matching (tag, indicators, required subfields)
- `TagRangeQuery` - Match fields in a tag range (e.g., 600-699 for all subjects)
- `SubfieldPatternQuery` - Regex matching on subfield values
- Record methods: `fields_matching()`, `fields_by_indicator()`, `fields_in_range()`

## Pymarc comparison

Pymarc only has `get_fields(*tags)` which filters by tag. Our Query DSL is a **value-add feature** beyond pymarc parity, offering:
- Indicator filtering (e.g., find 650 fields with indicator2='0' for LCSH)
- Tag range queries (e.g., all 6XX subject fields)
- Required subfield filtering
- Regex pattern matching

## Implementation approach

1. Create Python wrapper classes in src-python/src/:
   - `PyFieldQuery` with builder methods
   - Expose as `mrrc.FieldQuery`

2. Add methods to `PyRecord`:
   - `fields_matching(query)` - Returns list of fields matching query
   - `fields_by_indicator(tag, ind1=None, ind2=None)` - Convenience method
   - `fields_in_range(start_tag, end_tag)` - Tag range query

3. Ensure Pythonic API:
   - Use keyword arguments where appropriate
   - Return Python lists (not iterators) for simplicity
   - Good docstrings with examples

## Acceptance criteria

- Python users can construct FieldQuery objects
- Record.fields_matching() works with query objects
- Convenience methods for common patterns
- Tests in tests/python/ demonstrating usage
- Docstrings with examples
close_reason: Implemented Python bindings for Query DSL: FieldQuery, TagRangeQuery, SubfieldPatternQuery, SubfieldValueQuery with Record methods (fields_matching, fields_by_indicator, fields_in_range, fields_matching_pattern, fields_matching_value). Added 42 comprehensive tests.

title: Fix Phase 2: Use Python::assume_gil_acquired() instead of bound py.allow_threads()
description:
CRITICAL FIX per GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md Part 2 Fix 2 (lines 149-235):

The issue: py.allow_threads() on a bound Python<'py> handle (from PyRefMut) does not actually release the GIL.

The solution: Use Python::assume_gil_acquired() to get an unbound Python handle that properly supports GIL release.

Required Changes:
1. In PyMARCReader::__next__(), replace:
   let py = slf.py();  // WRONG - bound handle
   
   With:
   let py = unsafe { Python::assume_gil_acquired() };  // CORRECT - unbound handle

2. Safety guarantee: PyRefMut guarantees GIL is held, so assume_gil_acquired() is safe

3. This matches the exact pattern in the implementation plan (lines 187-189)

Expected Outcome:
- GIL will actually be released during Phase 2 parsing
- Threading speedup will jump from 0.83x to 1.8x+
- Two threads will execute truly concurrently

Files to modify:
- src-python/src/readers.rs (__next__ method)
close_reason: Applied critical GIL release fix per GIL_RELEASE_IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md Part 2 Fix 2. Changed __next__() to use Python::assume_gil_acquired() instead of slf.py().

title: Fix incomplete GIL release for file-path threading
description:
Phase 1 (read_next_record_bytes) is executed inside Python::with_gil(), keeping the GIL held during disk I/O. This serializes file reads across threads.

Current behavior:
- Thread 1: Acquire GIL → Read file (I/O) → Release GIL → Parse
- Threads 2-4: Waiting for GIL

Fix required:
- Refactor read_record() in readers.rs to split Phase 1 outside GIL for RustFile backend
- Phase 1 reads for RustFile should execute without GIL
- Phase 1 reads for PythonFile still need GIL (for .read() method)
- Phases 2-3 (parse + convert) stay the same

Impact:
- Current: 0.85x slowdown (454ms vs 386ms) for 4 concurrent file reads
- Expected after fix: 3.74x speedup as documented in RESULTS.md

See: src-python/src/readers.rs:109-155 (read_record), src-python/src/buffered_reader.rs:107-165 (read_next_record_bytes)
notes:
OBSOLETE: This issue was based on misunderstanding that standard MARCReader should support multi-threading via GIL release. Clarified architecture shows:

1. Standard MARCReader is sequential-only (7.5x faster single-threaded) ✅
2. Multi-threading uses separate ProducerConsumerPipeline API
3. Phase 1 GIL release in read_record() unnecessary for standard use case

Real work is in mrrc-0p0 (fix ProducerConsumerPipeline implementation).

title: Document and improve Python test separation: fast unit tests vs slow benchmarks
description:
## Analysis

The Python test suite already has marker-based separation, but it's not well-documented or integrated into the developer workflow.

### Current State

| Test Set | Tests | Duration |
|----------|-------|----------|
| All Python tests | 375 | ~223s (3:43) |
| Non-benchmark (`-m "not benchmark"`) | 314 | ~6s |
| Benchmark only (`-m "benchmark"`) | 61 | ~221s |

**Existing infrastructure:**
- `conftest.py` defines `@pytest.mark.benchmark` and `@pytest.mark.slow` markers
- Benchmark tests are properly marked in `test_benchmark_*.py` and `test_memory_benchmarks.py`
- `tests/python/README.md` documents the markers

**Gap:**
- `.cargo/check.sh` runs only 2 specific test files, not using the marker system
- Developers may not know about `-m "not benchmark"` for quick feedback
- No convenience commands for common workflows

### Proposed Approach

1. **Update `.cargo/check.sh`** to use marker-based selection:
   ```bash
   # Fast unit tests (default for pre-push)
   python -m pytest tests/python/ -m "not benchmark" -q
   ```

2. **Add convenience scripts or aliases** in pyproject.toml:
   ```toml
   [tool.pytest.ini_options]
   markers = [
       "benchmark: mark test as a benchmark",
       "slow: mark test as slow",
   ]
   addopts = "--ignore=.venv"
   ```

3. **Document in CONTRIBUTING.md or AGENTS.md**:
   - `pytest tests/python/ -m "not benchmark"` - Fast tests (~6s)
   - `pytest tests/python/ -m "benchmark"` - Benchmarks only (~4min)
   - `pytest tests/python/` - Everything (~4min)

4. **Consider adding pytest aliases** via pyproject.toml or Makefile:
   - `make test-py-fast` or `poe test-fast`
   - `make test-py-bench` or `poe test-bench`

### Acceptance Criteria
- Developers can easily run fast tests (~6s) during development
- Full benchmark suite remains runnable
- Commands are documented and discoverable
- `.cargo/check.sh` uses the optimized approach
close_reason: Implemented marker-based test separation: check.sh now uses -m 'not benchmark' to run 314 core tests in ~6s while excluding 61 benchmark tests. Updated pyproject.toml, AGENTS.md, and CI workflows.

title: Support control field access via record['001'].value pattern (pymarc compatibility)

title: Align Leader position value descriptions with LOC MARC 21 spec
description:
## Problem

The Leader `valid_values_at_position()` descriptions don't fully align with the official LOC MARC 21 specification at https://www.loc.gov/marc/bibliographic/bdleader.html

### Issues Found

**Position 6 (Type of record):**
- Missing: `t` should be "Manuscript language material" not "Text" (per full spec, "Text" is not in the list)

**Position 7 (Bibliographic level):**
- `a` should be "Monographic component part" (we have "Language material" which is wrong - copied from position 6)
- `b` missing: "Serial component part"
- `m` should be "Monograph/Item" (we have "Monograph")

**Position 17 (Encoding level):**
- `5` should be "Partial (preliminary) level" (we have "Partial level")

**Position 18 (Descriptive cataloging form):**
- Missing `c`: "ISBD punctuation omitted"
- Missing `n`: "Non-ISBD punctuation omitted"

### Reference

LOC MARC 21 Leader spec: https://www.loc.gov/marc/bibliographic/bdleader.html

### Fix

1. Update `src/leader.rs` `valid_values_at_position()` to match spec exactly
2. Update Python tests in `test_leader_values.py` to match
3. Ensure sentence case (first letter capitalized) matches LOC formatting

### Note on pymarc compatibility

pymarc does NOT have `get_valid_values()` or `describe_value()` methods - these are mrrc enhancements. So there's no pymarc API to deviate from; we should align with the authoritative LOC spec.
close_reason:
Aligned Leader position value descriptions with LOC MARC 21 spec:
- Position 6: Fixed 't' to 'Manuscript language material', removed obsolete 'h' (Microform)
- Position 7: Fixed 'a' to 'Monographic component part', added 'b' (Serial component part), fixed 'm' to 'Monograph/Item'

title: Support control field access via record['001'].value pattern
description:
**pymarc compatibility**: Support accessing control fields via record['001'].value pattern in addition to record.control_field('001').

**pymarc pattern**:
```python
record['001'].value  # For control fields
```

**Current mrrc**:
- record.control_field('001') returns string directly
- record['001'] might not have a .value property

**Implementation**:
- When record['tag'] returns a field for control fields (001-009), it should be a Field-like object with a .value property
- For data fields, continue returning Field wrapper as currently implemented
- Maintain backward compatibility: record.control_field('001') should still work

**Files**: mrrc/__init__.py (Record class)
**Related**: mrrc-5mn (API parity review), docs/design/PYMARC_API_PARITY_PLAN.md

**Testing**:
- test_control_field_value_access() - record['001'].value pattern
- test_control_field_backward_compat() - record.control_field() still works
- test_chained_access() - record['001'].value == record.control_field('001')
close_reason: Completed - Implemented ControlField wrapper with .value property for control field access via record['001'].value pattern

title: Implement Leader position-based access (__getitem__, __setitem__) for pymarc compatibility
description:
**pymarc compatibility gap**: Leader supports both property-based and position-based access patterns.

**pymarc patterns**:
```python
leader[0:5]          # Record length as string
leader[5]            # Record status character
leader[18]           # Cataloging form character
leader[5] = 'a'      # Set by position
```

**Current mrrc**:
- Only property-based access: leader.record_status
- No position-based access (leader[5])
- No slice support (leader[0:5])

**Implementation**:
- Add __getitem__ to Leader class that handles:
  - Single index: leader[5] returns string character at position
  - Slice: leader[0:5] returns substring
  - Raise IndexError for out-of-bounds
- Add __setitem__ for single position assignment
- Maintain all current property-based access as primary API
- Update type stubs in src-python/python/mrrc/__init__.pyi

**Files**: mrrc/__init__.py (Leader class), src-python/python/mrrc/__init__.pyi
**Related**: mrrc-5mn, docs/design/PYMARC_API_PARITY_PLAN.md

**Testing**:
- test_leader_single_position_access() - leader[5] patterns
- test_leader_slice_access() - leader[0:5] patterns
- test_leader_setitem() - leader[5] = 'a'
- Verify position/property access stay in sync
close_reason: Completed - Leader now supports both position-based and slice access (__getitem__, __setitem__)

title: Evaluate and implement calendar versioning (calver) scheme
description:
Evaluate switching from semantic versioning (0.3.1) to calendar versioning (calver) scheme and implement recommendation.

**Objective:** Determine if calver is appropriate for MRRC and establish versioning convention going forward.

**Calver Options to Evaluate:**

1. **YYYY.MM format** (e.g., 2026.01)
   - Simple, clear release dates
   - Good for time-based releases
   - Pros: Easy to understand when released
   - Cons: No patch version for hotfixes

2. **YYYY.MM.PATCH format** (e.g., 2026.01.1)
   - Calendar year/month + patch number
   - Allows hotfixes within month
   - Pros: Full flexibility with date clarity
   - Cons: Slightly more complex

3. **YYYY.MM.0 variants** (e.g., 2026.01.0, 2026.01.1)
   - Same as above with leading zero for patch
   - Pros: Consistent zero-padding
   - Cons: Slightly verbose

4. **Keep semantic versioning**
   - Current approach (0.3.1)
   - Pros: Industry standard, familiar to Rust ecosystem
   - Cons: Doesn't clearly indicate release date

**Evaluation Criteria:**

1. Rust ecosystem expectations
   - How other libraries handle versioning
   - Cargo and crates.io conventions
   - SemVer vs calver adoption in Rust

2. MRRC project characteristics
   - Rapid development cycle (suitable for calver)
   - Breaking vs non-breaking changes frequency
   - Release cadence preferences

3. User experience
   - Clarity of version numbers
   - Ease of understanding release timeline
   - Migration path from current 0.3.x

4. Tooling compatibility
   - Cargo.toml handling
   - CI/CD pipeline updates
   - Documentation updates needed

**Deliverables:**

1. Research document comparing options with pros/cons
2. Recommendation with rationale
3. Implementation plan including:
   - Update Cargo.toml and src-python/Cargo.toml
   - Update CHANGELOG.md versioning format
   - Update versioning documentation (if exists)
   - Update CI/CD version handling
   - Determine first version under new scheme
   - Create migration guide for users

**Consider:**
- Whether to retroactively convert existing versions
- How to handle the transition (0.3.1 → calver start)
- Documentation of versioning rationale in README/docs

title: Create \ pymarc compatibility test suite
description:
**Objective**: Create comprehensive test suite validating mrrc behavior against pymarc API patterns.

**Test Coverage**:

1. **Field access patterns** (test_field_compatibility.py)
   - field['a'] returns value or None (not KeyError)
   - field.get('a') returns value or default
   - field.get_subfields('a', 'b') returns list
   - field['z'] returns None for missing subfield
   - field in 'a' (check existence)

2. **Record access patterns** (test_record_compatibility.py)
   - record['245'] returns Field or None
   - record['999'] returns None
   - record['245']['a'] chains correctly
   - record['001'].value accesses control field
   - '245' in record checks existence

3. **Leader access patterns** (test_leader_compatibility.py)
   - leader.record_status property access
   - leader[5] position-based access
   - leader[0:5] slice access
   - leader.record_length == int(leader[0:5])
   - Position and property access stay in sync

4. **Round-trip compatibility** (test_roundtrip.py)
   - Write record with mrrc.MARCWriter
   - Read back with mrrc.MARCReader
   - Compare with original using field/subfield access patterns
   - Verify all None-returning patterns work throughout

5. **Reader/Writer patterns** (test_reader_writer.py)
   - Context manager support (with statement)
   - Iterator protocol
   - read_record() method
   - write_record() method

**Success Criteria**:
- All tests pass

- Tests can run independently and in parallel

title: Measure and document ISO 2709 baseline performance
description:

- Read throughput (records/sec): deserialize ISO 2709 → MarcRecord
- Write throughput (records/sec): serialize MarcRecord → ISO 2709
- File size (bytes): raw uncompressed

- Compression ratio: (1 - gzip_size / raw_size) × 100%
- Peak memory (MB): maximum resident set size during operation

**Environment to document:**
- CPU model, cores, architecture
- RAM available
- Storage type (SSD/HDD)
- OS version
- Rust version
- mrrc version/commit

**Output format:**
- Permanent document at docs/design/format-research/BASELINE_ISO2709.md
- Used as reference for all subsequent format evaluations
- Prevents retroactive baseline adjustments
